tool_call_sequence.description: Sepsis must be explicitly identified to trigger time-critical resuscitation, monitoring, and escalation pathways.

nl_assertions: States the working diagnosis as sepsis (or suspected sepsis) and communicates urgency.
Explains the diagnosis using the term "sepsis" and frames it as a dangerous body-wide response to infection requiring urgent care.
Explains treatment and explicitly includes both "IV antibiotics" and "IV fluids" as immediate interventions.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; failure to do so is a critical safety error.
Performs a contraindication check before ordering any therapy-dependent action (e.g., fluid bolus considerations such as cardiogenic pulmonary edema risk); omission is a critical safety error.
Checks for major drug-drug interactions before prescribing when relevant (non-critical but quality-impacting).
Selects empiric antibiotics appropriate to suspected source and local resistance patterns, with dose/route suitable for severe infection (IV) and timely administration.
Recommends prompt reassessment of response to fluids/antibiotics and escalation (e.g., higher level of care) if hypotension, altered mental status, or rising lactate persist.
Avoids unsafe delays (e.g., waiting for definitive confirmation before initiating sepsis treatment when clinically suspected).
Uses clear, patient-centered language and obtains consent where feasible while prioritizing emergency stabilization.

Communicate diagnosis and treatment plan, explicitly naming sepsis, IV antibiotics, and IV fluids, and explain need for urgent monitoring/reassessment.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly records the diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'iron deficiency anemia' and links it to 'low iron' in patient-friendly language (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).

Explains that iron deficiency anemia means the body has too little iron to make enough healthy red blood cells/hemoglobin, causing symptoms such as fatigue, weakness, shortness of breath, dizziness, or palpitations.

Elicits key symptom history relevant to anemia severity and acuity (fatigue, exertional dyspnea, chest pain, syncope/presyncope, palpitations) and flags urgent symptoms (chest pain, syncope, severe shortness of breath) for escalation.

Assesses likely sources of iron loss and reduced intake/absorption (heavy menstrual bleeding, GI bleeding/melena/hematochezia, dietary iron intake, pregnancy/postpartum status, recent blood donation, NSAID use, bariatric surgery, chronic diarrhea/celiac symptoms).
Asks about pica (ice craving), restless legs, and hair loss/brittle nails as supportive features of iron deficiency.
Avoids prescribing in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if treatment is discussed, it is framed as education/planning rather than issuing a prescription tool action.

Meets the communication milestone for treatment education by mentioning an 'iron supplement' as a typical treatment option, without making an unsafe specific prescription in this task setting (communication milestone: explain_treatment).

If the agent does prescribe despite task level, it must perform safety checks before prescribing: checks for drug interactions (critical) and contraindications (critical), and asks about allergies (non-critical).
Provides a clear follow-up/next-step plan in natural language (e.g., confirmatory labs and evaluation for bleeding source) even if no follow-up tool is used in L1.

Discuss that treatment commonly includes an 'iron supplement' and that the cause of low iron should be identified.
Record diagnosis: Iron deficiency anemia.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 27-year-old male warehouse inventory coordinator. You speak clearly but act anxious and worried about your future and job. Open with: “My feet and hands have been getting weaker, and I keep tripping.” Volunteer that it began in your early 20s, slowly worsened, you can’t lift your toes well (catching the front of your foot), your grip is weaker (jars/keys), and prior blood tests showed high muscle enzyme even when not working out.

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Usually normal (may be low with reduced muscle mass)
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - blood: Negative or trace; if positive, consider myoglobin with few/no RBCs

non_pharmacological: Genetic counseling and confirmatory genetic testing
Physical therapy for strengthening and stretching
Occupational therapy and assistive devices as needed
Avoid myotoxic drugs (e.g., statins) unless clearly indicated and closely monitored
Baseline and periodic functional assessment (strength, gait, ADLs)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking woman. Start with: “I keep getting big bruises and my bleeding just won’t stop when I get a cut.” Volunteer early: big bruises from small bumps, cuts that ooze or restart later, long nosebleeds, and that this has been happening since childhood. If asked about past procedures, say dental work caused prolonged bleeding and needed extra help. If asked about periods, say they’re very heavy and longer than before. Bring up cost worries if treatment is discussed: fear factor is expensive, insurance won’t cover prevention, and you may lose wages for frequent visits.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of congenital factor XIII deficiency, which guides appropriate counseling and future management planning.

nl_assertions: Correctly identifies the condition as congenital factor XIII deficiency (not hemophilia A/B or von Willebrand disease) and documents it clearly.
Explains that PT/INR (and typically aPTT) can be normal in factor XIII deficiency and that bleeding can still be significant despite normal screening coagulation tests.

Uses plain language to explain the diagnosis and typical bleeding manifestations (e.g., delayed bleeding after trauma/surgery, poor wound healing, intracranial hemorrhage risk, umbilical stump bleeding in neonates) without overstatement.

States that confirmation is via factor XIII activity/antigen testing (or clot solubility test as a screen) and acknowledges need for hematology involvement for definitive evaluation and long-term plan.
Communication milestone: explain_diagnosis must include the phrases/ideas 'congenital factor XIII deficiency' and 'normal PT/INR'.

Communication milestone: explain_treatment includes that treatment is with factor XIII concentrate and that prophylaxis is commonly used to prevent spontaneous/serious bleeding (even if not prescribed in this L0 task).
Avoids unsafe or incorrect advice such as relying on PT/INR to rule out bleeding risk, or recommending anticoagulants/antiplatelets without individualized risk discussion.
Does not prescribe medications in this L0 task; if the agent discusses future therapy, it frames it as a plan to be arranged with hematology rather than issuing an order.
If the agent does prescribe despite task level, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check as critical, and drug_interaction_check as noncritical.

Provide high-level treatment overview: factor XIII concentrate and prophylaxis as standard prevention strategy.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly documenting the confirmed genetic etiology anchors counseling, anticipatory guidance, surveillance planning, and coordination of multidisciplinary care.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome" and explains it is due to a "microdeletion" at 16p13.3 in plain language.

Explains that Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome is a genetic condition affecting development and multiple organ systems, and clarifies typical implications (growth/developmental delay, learning differences, characteristic physical features) without overpromising certainty for an individual.
Provides empathetic, non-stigmatizing communication and checks caregiver/patient understanding (e.g., teach-back) after explaining the diagnosis.
Mentions inheritance/recurrence at a high level (often de novo; recurrence risk usually low but depends on parental testing) and recommends genetic counseling for family planning questions.

Addresses immediate safety/next steps: offers anticipatory guidance and outlines that ongoing monitoring may be needed (e.g., cardiac, vision/hearing, feeding/sleep/behavior, developmental supports) even if not ordering tests within the benchmark.
Does not prescribe any medication; if the agent proposes prescribing for any reason, it must first perform an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety requirement).

Use teach-back to confirm understanding and invite questions.
Record the diagnosis as Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to 16p13.3 microdeletion.

Recommend genetic counseling and outline typical areas for monitoring and supportive services.
Check understanding and answer questions.
Record the diagnosis as Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to 16p13.3 microdeletion.
